title: Sedation Emergency – Anaphylaxis
description: A patient develops anaphylaxis during procedural sedation after propofol administration. Practise recognising anaphylaxis, delivering epinephrine as first-line treatment, and managing refractory anaphylactic shock.
learningObjectives: Recognise the signs and grading of anaphylaxis in a sedated patient
Administer intramuscular epinephrine as the first-line treatment
Understand why antihistamines and steroids are NOT first-line in anaphylaxis
Manage refractory anaphylactic shock with IV epinephrine infusion
Identify likely causative agents in procedural sedation anaphylaxis

states.prompt: Ninety seconds after propofol induction (200 mg), the patient develops: diffuse urticaria and flushing, BP drops from 128/76 to 72/40, HR rises from 72 to 128, SpO2 falls to 91% (bronchospasm audible). The patient received no other new medications. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: Anaphylaxis – IgE or non-IgE mediated severe hypersensitivity reaction
Vasovagal reaction
Propofol infusion syndrome
Oversedation with hypotension

explanations: Correct! Urticaria + hypotension + bronchospasm within 90 seconds of drug administration meets Sampson's criteria for Grade III–IV anaphylaxis.
Vasovagal reactions do not cause urticaria, bronchospasm, or this degree of haemodynamic collapse.
Propofol infusion syndrome occurs after prolonged infusion (>48 hrs) in ICU — not minutes after a bolus dose.
Oversedation causes respiratory depression and hypotension but NOT urticaria or bronchospasm.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies anaphylaxis from clinical triad

states.prompt: Anaphylaxis confirmed. The patient is hypotensive (BP 72/40) and in bronchospasm (SpO2 91%). What is the ABSOLUTE FIRST-LINE treatment?

options.label: Epinephrine 0.5 mg IM (anterolateral thigh) IMMEDIATELY
IV diphenhydramine 50 mg (antihistamine) first-line
IV hydrocortisone 200 mg to reduce late-phase reaction
1 L normal saline bolus to treat the hypotension first

explanations: Correct! Epinephrine 0.5 mg IM (1:1000 concentration, anterolateral thigh) is the ONLY first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Delays in epinephrine are the primary cause of anaphylaxis deaths.
Antihistamines treat urticaria but do NOT reverse hypotension, bronchospasm, or angioedema. They are adjunctive (2nd line) only.
Steroids prevent biphasic reactions but have NO effect for 4–6 hours. They are adjunctive (2nd line) only.
IV fluids support BP but do not treat anaphylaxis. Epinephrine FIRST, then fluids as adjunct for refractory hypotension.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies epinephrine IM as first-line treatment

states.prompt: Epinephrine 0.5 mg IM given. BP improving to 90/55. SpO2 95% on 15 L/min O2 via NRB mask. Urticaria persists. What ADJUNCT therapies are appropriate NOW?

options.label: IV fluid 1 L NS + IV diphenhydramine 50 mg + IV hydrocortisone 200 mg
IV diphenhydramine 50 mg only (antihistamine monotherapy)
Repeat epinephrine 0.5 mg IM again immediately (before assessing response)
Sodium bicarbonate IV to correct anaphylaxis-induced metabolic acidosis

explanations: Correct! After epinephrine, adjunctive therapy includes IV fluids (volume expansion), H1 antihistamine (urticaria), and steroids (biphasic prevention). All three are appropriate together.
Antihistamine alone is insufficient adjunct therapy. Fluids and steroids are also needed.
Repeat epinephrine should be given if there is no response or deterioration at 5–15 minutes. Do not repeat before assessing the first dose response.
Sodium bicarbonate is not indicated for anaphylaxis without confirmed severe metabolic acidosis with haemodynamic impact.

checklistItems.description: Correctly administers fluids, antihistamine, and steroids as adjuncts

states.prompt: Despite two IM epinephrine doses (5 min apart), BP remains 68/38. The patient is on IV fluids. This is refractory anaphylactic shock. What is the next step?

options.label: Epinephrine IV infusion (1–10 mcg/min titrated) or slow IV push 0.1–0.2 mg of 1:10,000 solution
A third dose of epinephrine 0.5 mg IM in the thigh
Start norepinephrine infusion as it is safer than IV epinephrine
Increase steroid dose to methylprednisolone 1 g IV

explanations: Correct! Refractory anaphylaxis with IV access requires IV epinephrine titration (infusion or careful slow IV push of diluted solution). IM absorption is unreliable in shock states.
IM absorption is unreliable in profound shock (vasoconstriction). Switch to IV route in refractory cases.
Norepinephrine lacks epinephrine's β2 bronchodilatory effect and is second-line. Epinephrine IV is the preferred agent for refractory anaphylaxis.
Steroids will not reverse refractory shock for hours. IV epinephrine is the immediate solution.

checklistItems.description: Escalates to IV epinephrine for refractory anaphylactic shock

states.prompt: BP stabilises to 105/65 after IV epinephrine. Bronchospasm resolved. The patient is drowsy but arousable. How long should this patient be monitored for biphasic anaphylaxis?

options.label: Minimum 4–6 hours in monitored setting; 12–24 hrs for severe or refractory reactions
1 hour is sufficient once BP normalises
No additional monitoring – epinephrine half-life covers the risk window
Admit for 1 week for allergy workup before discharge

explanations: Correct! Biphasic anaphylaxis (recurrence without re-exposure) occurs in 1–23% of cases, typically within 4–12 hours. Minimum 4–6 hours monitoring; 12–24 hours for severe/refractory cases.
1 hour is insufficient — biphasic reactions are well-documented up to 12 hours after the initial event.
Epinephrine's short half-life (minutes) means the patient is unprotected long before the biphasic risk window closes.
Allergy workup is important but can be arranged as an outpatient. 1-week inpatient admission is not standard of care.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies 4–6 hour minimum monitoring for biphasic anaphylaxis

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Anaphylaxis triad: urticaria + hypotension + bronchospasm after drug exposure
• Epinephrine IM 0.5 mg is THE first-line treatment – no delay
• Antihistamines and steroids are adjunctive only; they do NOT treat shock
• Refractory anaphylaxis: switch to IV epinephrine infusion
• Monitor ≥4–6 hours for biphasic reaction
